title: Chicken pathia
subtitle: Sweet, sour, and hot — the Persian-Parsi triple
excerpt: Chicken pieces in a thick, dark sauce that balances three competing elements: heat from chilli, sweetness from sugar, and sourness from tamarind. Pathia uses the same Parsi flavour architecture as dhansak but without the lentils — a cleaner, more concentrated sauce that coats the chicken and stains it deeply. Ordered for its distinctive triple-contrast character.
sourceNotes: Pathia (also patio) is a Parsi dish of Persian origin, a thick sweet-sour-hot sauce traditionally eaten with fish or meat. In the British curry-house it is typically made with chicken and ordered as an alternative to madras by diners who want heat alongside sweet-sour complexity.

content.text: Pathia is a balancing act: the three elements (sweet, sour, hot) need to be in tension with each other, not one dominating. Add the tamarind, then taste. Add the sugar, then taste again. They should counteract without cancelling. If the sauce tastes sweet, add more tamarind. If it tastes too sour, add more sugar. The heat from the chilli should be felt after swallowing, not immediately on the palate.

The sauce must be reduced until it is very thick and almost jammy before the chicken is returned. This concentration is what makes pathia different from a simple sweet-sour curry — the sauce clings to the chicken rather than surrounding it as a liquid.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over high heat. Brown the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} in batches for 2 minutes per side. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 10 minutes, stirring frequently, until reduced to a thick paste.

Add the {{tamarind-paste}} and {{brown-sugar}} to the tomato paste. Stir well and taste — adjust the balance so both sour and sweet are present. Add 200 ml water and bring to a simmer. Return the chicken and cook for 20 minutes until cooked through and the sauce is thick and clinging. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt.

items.fix: Taste at the stage before the chicken is returned. Add tamarind or sugar in small amounts until both are detectable but neither dominates

items.cause: Not reduced enough at the tomato-paste stage
items.fix: Simmer uncovered and stir frequently until the sauce thickens to coat the back of a spoon heavily
Thigh meat is better for this dish — it tolerates the long sauce reduction without drying. Reduce cooking time for breast pieces
Add a pinch more at the end — pathia should be hot, sitting in the same bracket as madras

content.text: Pathia is a regular on British curry-house menus though less commonly ordered than tikka masala or korma — it rewards diners who want something more complex than a standard hot curry. The sweet-sour-hot triad is a Parsi cooking principle that appears in multiple dishes; pathia is its cleanest expression on the British Indian menu. It is worth ordering with plain rice rather than pilau, so the sauce flavour is not competing with spiced rice.
